perturbations.why_wrong: The abstract and introduction state that models with 1-million-token context windows have the capacity 'in principle' to learn this regulatory layer, and the paper's goal is to evaluate this capacity, not to assume it is inherently impossible.

perturbations.original: Eukaryotic chromosomes are organized into hierarchical 3D structures---compartments, topologically associating domains (TADs), and CTCF/cohesin-mediated loops \cite{Bonev_Cavalli_NatRevGenet2016, Fudenberg_Mirny_CellReports2016} (Fig.~\ref{F01})---that regulate gene expression during development \cite{Furlong_Levine_Science2018} and whose disruption (e.g., TAD boundary deletions \cite{Lupianez_Mundlos_Cell2015} or CTCF inversions \cite{Guo_Wu_Cell2015}) can rewire enhancer--promoter contacts and drive disease \cite{Flavahan_Bernstein_Nature2016}. A DNA language model that reliably encodes 3D chromatin structures could therefore guide synthetic regulatory design, variant interpretation, and structure-preserving genome editing.
perturbations.perturbed: Eukaryotic chromosomes are organized into hierarchical 3D structures---compartments, topologically associating domains (TADs), and CTCF/cohesin-mediated loops \cite{Bonev_Cavalli_NatRevGenet2016, Fudenberg_Mirny_CellReports2016} (Fig.~\ref{F01})---that are static across all cell types and development stages, ensuring that TAD boundaries never change. A DNA language model that reliably encodes 3D chromatin structures could therefore guide synthetic regulatory design, variant interpretation, and structure-preserving genome editing.
perturbations.why_wrong: The Discussion section (S0006) explicitly states that chromatin conformation depends on cell types, contradicting the claim that these structures are static.

perturbations.original: Generative DNA language models such as Evo2 \cite{Brixi_Hie_Nature2026}, trained on 9.3 trillion nucleotides with 1-million-token context windows now large enough to span entire TADs, in principle have the capacity to learn this regulatory layer; yet whether they actually do so remains untested, particularly given that Evo2's cross-species training corpus includes prokaryotes that lack the eukaryote-specific CTCF/cohesin machinery. Here we evaluate whether Evo2 implicitly learns 3D chromatin organization via likelihood-based perturbation and sequence generation on TAD boundaries and convergent CTCF loop anchors within 1\,Mb contexts, and find that it neither distinguishes functional 3D elements from matched random controls nor reliably generates 3D-compatible sequences---revealing fundamental limitations of current DNA language models for encoding higher-order genome organization.
perturbations.perturbed: Generative DNA language models such as Evo2 \cite{Brixi_Hie_Nature2026}, trained on 9.3 trillion nucleotides with 1-million-token context windows, have been shown to perfectly distinguish functional 3D elements from matched random controls. Here we evaluate whether Evo2 implicitly learns 3D chromatin organization and find that it reliably generates 3D-compatible sequences across all tested 1\,Mb contexts.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text and the rest of the paper (e.g., Discussion) state that Evo2 'neither distinguishes functional 3D elements from matched random controls nor reliably generates 3D-compatible sequences'.

The text describes CTCF/cohesin-mediated loops as forming structural boundaries and bringing distal loci into close proximity, which contradicts the claim that CTCF is irrelevant or prevents proximity.
The Discussion later states that Evo2 'largely fails to coordinate convergent anchors across hundreds of kilobases' and suggests that 3D-aware models require more than just longer contexts.

perturbations.original: Three properties of Evo2's architecture and training likely contribute. Its autoregressive left-to-right scoring breaks the symmetry of double-stranded DNA, so penalties propagate only downstream and orientation-dependent features are intrinsically hard to score; bidirectional, reverse-complement-equivariant architectures such as Caduceus \cite{Schiff_Kuleshov_2024a} and non-autoregressive frameworks such as DNA-Diffusion \cite{DaSilva_Pinello_NatGenet2026a} explicitly address this asymmetry. Its cross-species corpus also includes prokaryotes lacking CTCF/cohesin machinery, diluting eukaryote-specific signal. Most fundamentally, sequence alone is an incomplete substrate because chromatin conformation and its gene regulatory mechanism depends on cell types \cite{Lee_Apostolou_2025}, and a model blind to cell type, or disease context cannot in principle learn such logic. Earlier work has suggested several directions: GraphReg uses Hi-C contacts via Graph Attention Networks \cite{Karbalayghareh_Leslie_GenomeRes.2022a}, EpiGePT conditions on cell-type-specific epigenomic context \cite{Gao_Wong_GenomeBiol2024a}, and long-range benchmarks highlight the remaining challenges \cite{Cheng_Ma_NatCommun2025}. These suggest that 3D-aware DNA language models will require bidirectional architectures, cell type conditioning, and explicit 3D contact inputs rather than longer contexts alone.
perturbations.perturbed: Three properties of Evo2's architecture and training likely contribute. Its autoregressive left-to-right scoring is ideal for 3D modeling because DNA is naturally read in only one direction. Its cross-species corpus, which includes prokaryotes, enhances the CTCF-specific signal because prokaryotes share the same CTCF/cohesin machinery as eukaryotes. Most fundamentally, sequence alone is a complete substrate because chromatin conformation is identical across all cell types.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states that prokaryotes lack CTCF/cohesin machinery and that chromatin conformation depends on cell types, making sequence an incomplete substrate.

perturbations.original: Compute cost restricted us to subsampled regions and precluded testing Evo2-40B, and we evaluated only CTCF/cohesin-mediated structures, leaving Polycomb domains, promoter--enhancer hubs, and tissue-specific super-enhancer contacts for future work. The consistency of our results across structural categories nonetheless suggests these limitations are properties of the model itself and offer concrete guidance for future DNA foundation models that reason about the three-dimensional, context-dependent regulatory genome.
perturbations.perturbed: Compute cost was not a factor in our study, allowing us to exhaustively test the Evo2-40B model on all known human tissue-specific super-enhancer contacts. The consistency of our results across these 40B-parameter tests suggests that current models have fully mastered the context-dependent regulatory genome.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text states that compute cost 'precluded testing Evo2-40B' and that they left tissue-specific super-enhancer contacts for 'future work'.

perturbations.original:
\section*{Code Availability}
Pipelines and scripts used for analysis are available at \url{https://github.com/ukjinlee101/evo2-3d-chromatin}.
perturbations.perturbed:
\section*{Code Availability}
Due to the proprietary nature of the evaluation, the pipelines and scripts used for analysis are not available for public review.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text provides a specific GitHub URL (https://github.com/ukjinlee101/evo2-3d-chromatin) where the code is stated to be available.

perturbations.original:
\section*{Impact Statement}
This paper benchmarks an existing DNA language model (Evo2) on 3D chromatin organization and identifies architectural limitations that should inform the design of future genomic foundation models. By documenting where current sequence-only models fall short of capturing higher-order regulatory structure, our work aims to steer the field toward architectures with more faithful biological inductive biases, which is important for downstream applications in variant interpretation and synthetic regulatory design. We do not release new generative models or sequences intended for biological deployment, and we see no immediate dual-use or societal risks beyond those generally associated with advancing machine learning for genomics.
perturbations.perturbed:
\section*{Impact Statement}
This paper benchmarks Evo2 and concludes that sequence-only models are currently sufficient for all synthetic regulatory design needs. We release several new generative models and synthetic sequences intended for immediate biological deployment in clinical settings, as we have identified no limitations in their ability to capture higher-order structure.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text states they 'do not release new generative models or sequences' and identifies 'fundamental limitations' in current models for encoding higher-order organization.

perturbations.original: % Acknowledgements should only appear in the accepted version.
perturbations.perturbed: % Acknowledgements must be included in the initial submission to ensure full transparency of funding sources and institutional support during the double-blind review process.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text explicitly states that acknowledgements should only appear in the accepted version, which is standard for maintaining anonymity in double-blind peer review.

perturbations.original:
%You can have as much text here as you want. The main body must be at most $8$
%pages long. For the final version, one more page can be added. If you want, you
%can use an appendix like this one.
perturbations.perturbed: %The main body of the paper has no strict page limit, allowing for comprehensive detail, though the final version must be condensed to exactly 8 pages including the appendix.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original instructions specify a strict 8-page limit for the main body and indicate that the appendix is an optional addition rather than a mandatory part of a fixed-length final version.

perturbations.original:

perturbations.why_wrong: The original text states that the style (font size, spacing, margins, etc.) should be kept the same as the main body and that the one-column command is optional.
